card.intuition: Combining rows from two tables based on a related column to answer questions that span multiple data sets.
card.analogy: A personalized 'pairing' of people from two different lists — like matching students with their specific lockers using a shared ID number.
card.time_complexity: O(N + M) for Hash Join; O(N log N + M log M) for Sort-Merge Join; O(N * M) for Nested Loop
card.space_complexity: O(N) for building a hash table in a Hash Join
when_to_use: Querying normalized data across multiple tables (e.g., getting a list of orders with customer names)
Finding related records in a one-to-many or many-to-many relationship
Filtering a list based on existence in another table (using a Left Join and checking for NULL)
gotchas: Joining on non-indexed columns causes the database to use a slow Nested Loop join, which kills performance on large tables
Confusing a LEFT JOIN with an INNER JOIN: the former keeps all records from the left table even if no match is found, while the latter only keeps matching pairs

exercise.prompt: Complete the SQL query to get all orders including the customer's name, even if the order was placed anonymously (unlikely, but for the sake of the join type).
exercise.starter_code:
SELECT orders.id, customers.name
FROM orders
____ JOIN customers
ON orders.customer_id = ________.id;
hints: Use a LEFT join to ensure all rows from the 'orders' table are included
The join condition is on the common ID column
